items.q: What did the original Phillips curve suggest, and how has thinking changed?
items.correct: The original Phillips curve suggested a stable inverse relationship between unemployment and inflation. Friedman and the 1970s stagflation experience added expectations — the long-run Phillips curve is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment.
items.misconception: The Phillips curve still proves you can always reduce unemployment by allowing more inflation.
items.random_wrong: The Phillips curve shows the link between phone use and unemployment.
items.why: The simple inverse trade-off broke down empirically. Modern theory distinguishes short-run (trade-off possible) from long-run (no trade-off). Students learn the original curve as if still valid.

items.correct: A sustained rise in the general price level over time — reducing the purchasing power of money. Measured by the percentage change in a price index.
items.misconception: Inflation is when prices are high.
items.random_wrong: Inflation is when the economy grows too fast.
items.why: Inflation is about RISING prices, not high prices. A country can have stable high prices (no inflation) or rising low prices (high inflation). Students confuse level with rate of change.

items.q: How does raising interest rates reduce inflation?
items.correct: Higher rates make borrowing more expensive and saving more attractive, reducing consumption and investment — lowering aggregate demand and slowing price rises.
items.misconception: Higher interest rates directly force shops to lower their prices.
items.random_wrong: Higher interest rates make money more valuable so inflation falls automatically.
items.why: The transmission runs through aggregate demand, not direct price control. Higher rates dampen spending, which cools demand-pull inflation. Students miss the demand-side mechanism.
The answer examiners want is: Inflation against unemployment. “Inflation against GDP” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In Phillips' data, low unemployment was associated with:
items.why: Correct answer: High inflation. “Low inflation” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Exact answer: 1958. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
